title: Dental Blocks: Are They Worth Doing？ | Dr Tim's Tips
filename: Dental Blocks： Are They Worth Doing？ ｜ Dr Tim_s Tips.en.vtt
excerpt: So the question are dental blocks worth doing very important [Music] and it depends a lot on presumably we're talking about lip treatments so if you can have a lip augmentation is it worth numbing the nerves around the mouth the person probably best to ask is someone who's experi…

segments.text: so the question are dental blocks worth doing very important [Music] and it depends a lot on presumably we're talking about lip treatments so if you can have a lip augmentation is it worth numbing the nerves around the mouth the person probably best to ask is someone who's experienced both so a patient who's had a dental block had a lip augmentation and a patient who hasn't had a dental block maybe had cream or some other source of anesthetic or maybe none at all and asked them what their experience was now when i did this with my patients most of the patients who'd had a dental block so they would rather not have one again and the reason for that is firstly the actual dental block is painful so when you're actually giving the injection there's a degree of discomfort obviously i know there are many dentists who will be saying oh that doesn't work hurt at all depends on the patient depends on the exact procedure but usually there's a degree of discomfort after that initial injection you still do the lip augmentation and a percentage of the time it doesn't numb the whole area anyway so you still have one or two injections that still hurt

that's another negative for dental blocks and then finally you've got the recovery period which means you might have numbness and droopiness being unable to speak or to you know eat anything for a couple of hours after and these are all the side effects that add up to the sense for many patients that all that stuff is not really worth it when you can have the whole procedure done maybe in as little as five to ten minutes and then it's over and your recovery is much faster there's also sometimes asymmetry of the muscles that's caused by a certain style of dental blocks and that can make assessing the treatment and the actual look of the treatment afterwards not as good so for all of those reasons there are many patients who are better off not having a dental block however there are those patients who are so anxious about pain that they simply won't have a procedure without one and that's probably the category of person that i'm most likely to do a dental block on is when they're absolutely terrified i do a little gingival block small amounts take the edge off it and that helps a little bit in those cases they may next time they come decide i'd rather not have that experience for the reasons i've already

said um but it's really that you're weighing up that they still cause a degree of pain there's a lot of recovery afterwards there's a somewhat decrease in the satisfaction of the result if you have asymmetry of the muscles or you can't eat or drink or speak properly but overall um those are the things to weigh up with your patient
